schools.summary: Paul's ringing exhortation, the conclusion of the allegory: 'stand fast (stand firm, hold your ground) in the liberty (freedom from the law's yoke) wherewith Christ hath made us free' — the freedom Christ purchased is to be guarded; 'be not entangled (held fast, ensnared) again with the yoke of bondage' — do not submit once more to the slavery of the law (circumcision and ceremonies) from which Christ freed them (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Christian liberty is freedom from the law, sin, and death as the ground of righteousness; to return to the law is to take up again an intolerable yoke.

themes: Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free
be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage
the call to stand fast in Christian liberty
freedom from the law's yoke

schools.summary: Paul speaks with the full weight of his apostolic authority: 'behold, I Paul say unto you' — a solemn personal asseveration — 'that if ye be circumcised (submit to it as necessary to justification), Christ shall profit you nothing'; to seek justification by circumcision (and so by the law) is to renounce Christ, for the two ways of justification cannot be combined; whoever trusts the law gains nothing from Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Paul will not allow the smallest mingling of law and gospel as the ground of righteousness; to add circumcision is to lose Christ entirely.

themes: if ye be circumcised, Christ shall profit you nothing
Paul's solemn warning that circumcision nullifies Christ
the incompatibility of law and grace
the either/or of justification

schools.summary: Paul's testimony: 'every man that is circumcised (as a means of justification) is a debtor (bound, obligated) to do the whole law' — to accept circumcision on that ground is to assume the obligation to keep the entire Mosaic law; the crushing consequence being that one who would be justified by the law must keep all of it perfectly (not part, but the whole), and since none can, this way leads only to the curse (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the Judaizers offered circumcision as a small thing, but it binds the conscience to the impossible burden of the entire law; there is no partial keeping.

themes: every man that is circumcised, that he is a debtor to do the whole law
circumcision binds one to keep the whole law
the law's total demand
no partial keeping

schools.summary: Those who 'would be justified by the law' are 'severed from Christ' (made void of him, abolished from him), to seek righteousness by law being to render Christ useless to oneself, leaving the sphere of grace for the sphere of law. Barnes: the statement is conditional and hypothetical — 'grace' means the system of justification by grace, not personal saving religion; since Paul elsewhere denies anyone is in fact justified by the law (Romans 3:20), the verse cannot prove that a true believer has fallen away, only that to adopt the legal scheme is to reject the gospel of grace. Christ will be a whole Saviour or none (Robertson: a serious matter of substituting law for Christ as the agent in salvation).

Calvin: to seek any part of righteousness in the works of the law is to renounce Christ and treat his grace as of no value; even the smallest part of justification cannot be attributed to the law without renouncing Christ — 'grace' here is the gracious gospel covenant, not a personal state that the elect finally lose.

Clarke (with Wesley): they had been brought into the grace of the gospel and now, by readopting the Mosaic ordinances, had apostatized from it and lost the grace communicated to their souls — 'whether some of them ever rose again is more than we can tell'; Wesley: 'ye renounce the new covenant,' a real falling from grace.

debates.question: Does 'ye are fallen from grace' prove that a true believer can finally fall away?

sides.position: No — it is conditional/hypothetical; 'grace' is the gospel system of justification, renounced by adopting the law (not proof of apostasy from saving grace)
Yes — a real falling from the saving grace once received, by readopting the law

themes: Christ is become of no effect unto you, whosoever of you are justified by the law
ye are fallen from grace
Christ made void and the fall from grace
the perseverance crux

schools.summary: Paul sets the true way over against the legal: 'we (true believers) through the Spirit (not the flesh or the law) wait for (eagerly expect) the hope of righteousness by faith' — the full hope that righteousness by faith secures; 'the hope of righteousness' being either the hoped-for blessings that flow from being justified (the final glory) or righteousness itself as the object of hope; believers, already justified by faith, await the consummation of that hope (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the Christian life is one of waiting in hope, by the Spirit and through faith — not earning by works, but expecting the promised righteousness and glory.

themes: we through the Spirit wait for the hope of righteousness by faith
believers waiting through the Spirit
the hope of righteousness by faith
the life of faith and hope

schools.summary: 'In Jesus Christ' (in the Christian state, in the matter of justification) 'neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision' — the outward rite, kept or unkept, contributes nothing to one's standing with God; the one thing that counts is 'faith which worketh (energoumene — is energized, operative, active) by love' — a living faith that expresses itself in love; justifying faith is never idle but works through love, love being the fruit and evidence of faith, not the ground of justification (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin): faith alone justifies, yet true faith is not dead but bears fruit in love; works and love follow faith as its necessary effect, not its cause — against the reading that faith is 'formed' or made justifying by love (fides caritate formata).

themes: neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision; but faith which worketh by love
in Christ only faith working by love avails
love the fruit and evidence of faith
faith never idle

schools.summary: The racing metaphor: 'ye did run well' — the Galatians had begun the Christian course with vigor and good progress — 'who did hinder (enekopsen — cut in on, impede, as a runner cutting across the track) you that ye should not obey the truth?' The pointed question implies that someone had wrongfully interfered — the false teachers — who had blocked their progress and turned them from obedience to the truth of the gospel (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Luther (with Calvin): it pains the apostle that those who ran so well should be tripped up by the Judaizers; good beginnings are no security against being hindered.

themes: Ye did run well; who did hinder you that ye should not obey the truth
Paul asking who hindered their good progress
the false teachers as the culprits
good runners tripped up

schools.summary: Paul declares that 'this persuasion (the influence that turned them, the new conviction toward the law) cometh not of him that calleth you' — it does not come from God, who had called them to the gospel of grace; God, the author of their calling to liberty and faith, is not the source of this legalistic persuasion, which comes from elsewhere — from the false teachers, and ultimately from the enemy (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): whatever turns the soul from the gospel of grace is not of God, however plausible; the call of God is always to Christ and freedom, never to the bondage of the law.

themes: This persuasion cometh not of him that calleth you
their false persuasion not from God
God's call is to grace, not bondage
the alien source of the error

schools.summary: Paul cites the proverb (as in 1 Corinthians 5:6): 'a little leaven leaveneth (ferments, pervades) the whole lump' — a small amount of leaven spreads through and transforms the entire batch of dough; the warning being that a little false doctrine, like a little leaven, will spread and corrupt the whole church; the small beginning of the Judaizing error, if tolerated, would pervade and ruin the whole body of believers (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): no error in the doctrine of justification is small or harmless; the least corruption of the gospel, like leaven, works through the whole and must be purged.

themes: A little leaven leaveneth the whole lump
a little error corrupts the whole
false doctrine spreading like leaven
the peril of tolerating error

schools.summary: Paul tempers his rebuke with hope: 'I have confidence in you through the Lord (trusting in the Lord's working in them) that ye will be none otherwise minded' — that they will return to the truth and not adopt the error; and the warning against the false teacher: 'he that troubleth (unsettles, disturbs) you shall bear his judgment (the penalty, the condemnation), whosoever he be' — however prominent, the troubler will answer to God (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Paul hopes the best for the deceived sheep but pronounces judgment on the deceiver; mercy for the led-astray, severity for the leader.

themes: I have confidence in you through the Lord, that ye will be none otherwise minded
he that troubleth you shall bear his judgment, whosoever he be
Paul's confidence and warning
mercy for the misled, threat for the misleader

schools.summary: Paul rebuts a slander (that he himself still preached circumcision): 'if I yet preach circumcision, why do I yet suffer persecution?' — his continued persecution by the Jews proves he does not, for they would not persecute one who upheld the law; if he preached circumcision, 'the offence (skandalon, stumbling-block) of the cross' would have 'ceased' — the cross is an offence to the Jews precisely because it sets aside the law as the way of salvation, so were he to preach the law, the offence (and his persecution) would vanish (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the preaching of the cross, of free justification apart from the law, always gives offence and draws persecution; to remove the offence is to corrupt the gospel.

themes: if I yet preach circumcision, why do I yet suffer persecution
then is the offence of the cross ceased
Paul's persecution proving he preaches not circumcision
the offence of the cross

schools.summary: Paul's vehement words against the false teachers: 'I would they were even cut off (apokopsontai) which trouble you' — most take it as a sharp play on circumcision, wishing that those who press cutting (circumcision) would go further and cut (mutilate) themselves off entirely; a stern, ironic word of indignation against the troublers; some read it as a wish they be 'cut off' (excommunicated, removed) from the church, but all agree it is an expression of strong indignation against those who unsettle the church (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Paul's burning concern for the gospel and the souls of the Galatians makes him speak with vehemence against the deceivers; holy zeal does not mince words against error.

themes: I would they were even cut off which trouble you
Paul's sharp wish concerning the troublers
the play on circumcision
holy zeal against error

schools.summary: Paul affirms the Galatians' calling: they 'have been called unto liberty' — freedom from the law's yoke — but this liberty has limits and a right use; it is not to be made 'an occasion (aphorme — a pretext, a base of operations) to the flesh' (Christian freedom is not license to sin, nor an excuse for fleshly indulgence); rather 'by love serve (douleuete — be slaves to) one another' — the paradox that the free are to make themselves servants of each other through love (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Wesley (with Calvin): true liberty expresses itself in mutual, loving service; the believer freed from the law's bondage willingly becomes the servant of his brethren in love.

themes: ye have been called unto liberty; only use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh
by love serve one another
liberty used in loving service
freedom not license

schools.summary: Paul declares that 'all the law is fulfilled (summed up and accomplished) in one word (one saying, one precept)' — the whole of the law's requirement toward others is gathered into a single command: 'Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself' (Leviticus 19:18, as Christ taught, Matthew 22:39); love is the fulfilling of the law, for he who truly loves his neighbour does all that the law requires toward him (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the freedom from the law as a means of justification does not abolish love; the free man, serving by love (v.13), fulfils the very law's intent — love does what law commanded.

themes: all the law is fulfilled in one word... Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself
the whole law fulfilled in love of neighbour
love the fulfilling of the law
liberty fulfilling the law's intent

schools.summary: Paul's vivid warning, the dark opposite of loving service: 'if ye bite and devour one another' — the figure of wild beasts tearing each other — picturing the strife, contention, and mutual injury that the Galatians' disputes were producing; the dire result, 'take heed that ye be not consumed (destroyed, eaten up) one of another' — such mutual biting will end in the ruin of all; contention devours a church as beasts devour one another (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): the legalistic controversies had bred bitter strife; Paul warns that this party spirit, if unchecked, will destroy the whole community.

themes: if ye bite and devour one another, take heed that ye be not consumed one of another
the warning against mutual destruction
contention devouring the church
strife ending in ruin

schools.summary: Paul's positive direction: 'walk in (by, according to) the Spirit' — order the whole conduct of life by the guidance and power of the Holy Spirit; the Spirit-led walk is the remedy against the flesh; and the assured result, 'ye shall not fulfil (carry out, gratify) the lust (desire, craving) of the flesh' — walking by the Spirit, the believer will not give in to the cravings of his corrupt nature, the Spirit overcoming the flesh (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Henry): the flesh is not eradicated but overcome; the way to defeat its lusts is not the law but the indwelling Spirit, by whose power the believer walks in holiness.

themes: Walk in the Spirit, and ye shall not fulfil the lust of the flesh
the remedy: walk in the Spirit
the Spirit overcoming the flesh
holiness by the Spirit, not the law

schools.summary: Paul describes the internal warfare: 'the flesh (the corrupt nature) lusteth (wars, strives with desire) against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh' — two opposed principles in conflict within the believer; they are 'contrary (opposed, set against) one another,' their aims and desires wholly antagonistic, no peace between them in this life; 'so that ye cannot do the things that ye would' — each checking the other, the Spirit restraining the flesh's evil and the flesh hindering the Spirit's good, so neither has free, unhindered course (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Luther): the regenerate are not wholly free from the flesh in this life; the Spirit checks the flesh, so that the believer cannot do the full evil the flesh would, yet the flesh hinders, so that he cannot do the full good the Spirit would — the conflict marks the believer, not the unregenerate.

themes: the flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh
these are contrary the one to the other: so that ye cannot do the things that ye would
the warfare of flesh and Spirit
the inner conflict of the believer

schools.summary: Paul's conclusion: 'if ye be led of (by) the Spirit (governed and guided by the Holy Spirit), ye are not under the law' — those whom the Spirit leads are free from the law's bondage and condemnation; 'under the law' meaning under its yoke, its curse, and its condemning power, from which the Spirit-led believer is delivered, walking in the freedom of grace, the law's righteous requirement being fulfilled in him by the Spirit (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin): the believer led by the Spirit need not fear the law, for he is no longer under it as a slave but walks in the liberty of the gospel; the Spirit's leading and the law's bondage are mutually exclusive.

themes: if ye be led of the Spirit, ye are not under the law
those led by the Spirit not under the law
deliverance from the law's yoke and curse
the freedom of the Spirit-led

schools.summary: Paul lists 'the works of the flesh' — the deeds the corrupt nature produces — which are 'manifest' (open, evident, plainly recognizable as evil); he begins with the sins of sensuality: 'adultery, fornication (sexual sins), uncleanness (impurity of every kind), lasciviousness (aselgeia — wantonness, shameless indulgence)' — the gross works of corrupt desire (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): Paul sets the dark catalogue of the flesh's works over against the fruit of the Spirit (v.22), that believers may know and shun what the flesh produces.

themes: the works of the flesh are manifest... Adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lasciviousness
the works of the flesh
the sins of impurity
the manifest deeds of the corrupt nature

schools.summary: Paul continues the catalogue: 'idolatry (false worship, the worship of idols) and witchcraft (pharmakeia — sorcery, dealing with evil powers and drugs)' — the sins against true religion — followed by the sins of contention: 'hatred, variance (strife, quarrels), emulations (jealousy), wrath (outbursts of anger), strife (factions, self-seeking), seditions (divisions, dissensions), heresies (parties, sects)' — the social sins that tear apart fellowship (Barnes, Ellicott).
Wesley: the flesh produces not only fleshly lusts but sins of the temper and spirit — pride, anger, party-spirit — which are no less its works than sensuality.

themes: Idolatry, witchcraft, hatred, variance, emulations, wrath, strife, seditions, heresies
the works of the flesh in false worship and contention
sins against religion and against others
the divisive works of the flesh

schools.summary: The list ends with 'envyings, murders, drunkenness, revellings (komoi — carousing, riotous feasting), and such like' — the catalogue not exhaustive but representative of the flesh's works; Paul's earnest forewarning, repeated as before: 'they which do (prassontes — practise, habitually) such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God' — those whose lives are characterized by these works of the flesh are excluded from the kingdom (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Luther, Calvin): this is no idle threat; the habitual practice of the flesh's works is incompatible with saving faith and bars from the inheritance — a sobering warning even to professing believers.

themes: Envyings, murders, drunkenness, revellings, and such like
they which do such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God
the warning against the flesh's doers
exclusion from the kingdom

schools.summary: The deliberate contrast with 'the works of the flesh': the Spirit's product is called 'fruit' (karpos — singular, one organic cluster, the natural growth of the Spirit's life) not 'works'; it is what the indwelling Spirit produces in the believer; the graces beginning with 'love (the root of all), joy, peace (toward God and man), longsuffering (patience under wrong), gentleness (chrestotes — kindness), goodness (benevolence), faith (pistis — fidelity, faithfulness)' — the beautiful character the Spirit forms (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Luther, Calvin): these graces are not the achievements of human effort or the law but the fruit of the Holy Spirit dwelling in the heart; they grow where the Spirit reigns.

themes: the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith
the fruit of the Spirit
the cluster of graces
the Spirit's character formed in the believer

schools.summary: The catalogue of the Spirit's fruit closes with 'meekness (praotes — gentleness of spirit, humility) and temperance (enkrateia — self-control, mastery of the appetites)' — completing the nine graces; and the striking conclusion 'against such there is no law' — no law condemns these graces; the law has nothing to say against love, joy, peace, and the rest, for they are the very things the law aimed at; the Spirit's fruit fulfils, and stands above, the law (Barnes, Robertson).

Luther (with Calvin): the believer who bears the Spirit's fruit needs no law to compel or condemn him, for he does freely and from the heart what the law could only command; the law is not against, but for, such conduct.

themes: Meekness, temperance: against such there is no law
the fruit completed and free from the law
the Spirit's fruit fulfils the law
no law against the graces

schools.summary: Paul's declaration: 'they that are Christ's (those who truly belong to him) have crucified (put to death, as on a cross) the flesh with the affections (passions) and lusts (desires)' — believers have decisively renounced and put to death the corrupt nature; the figure of crucifixion: as Christ was crucified, so the believer, united to him, has crucified the flesh — a decisive, deliberate putting to death of the sinful nature, begun in conversion and continued through life (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): this is the distinguishing mark of those who are Christ's — not perfection, but the daily crucifying of the flesh; the believer wars against and mortifies his corrupt desires.

themes: they that are Christ's have crucified the flesh with the affections and lusts
Christ's people have crucified the flesh
the decisive putting to death of the corrupt nature
the mark of believers

schools.summary: Paul's logic: 'if we live in (by) the Spirit (if the Spirit is the source of our spiritual life) let us also walk in (by) the Spirit (stoichomen — walk in line, keep step)' — our daily conduct should match the source of our life; profession must issue in practice. To 'live' by the Spirit is to have spiritual life from him (the inward principle); to 'walk' by the Spirit is to order one's outward conduct by him (the practical course) — the two must agree (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): those who have received the Spirit's life are to make it visible in a Spirit-led walk; the indicative (we live by the Spirit) grounds the imperative (let us walk by the Spirit).

themes: If we live in the Spirit, let us also walk in the Spirit
the call to walk by the Spirit by whom we live
living and walking by the Spirit
profession issuing in practice

schools.summary: Paul's closing exhortation of the chapter: 'let us not be desirous of vain glory (kenodoxoi — empty, conceited glory-seeking, craving the praise of men)' — the root of much strife — with its two consequences: 'provoking (challenging, irritating) one another' (the stronger inciting the weaker) and 'envying one another' (the weaker resenting the stronger); both spring from the empty pursuit of glory (Barnes, Robertson).
Luther (with Calvin): vainglory was at the root of the Galatians' divisions and the false teachers' ambition; the cure for the strife that devours a church is the renouncing of empty self-seeking.

themes: Let us not be desirous of vain glory, provoking one another, envying one another
the warning against vainglory, provocation, and envy
vainglory the root of strife
renouncing empty self-seeking
